fixture.description: squaraine quantum yields paragraph kept_origin drift

snapshot.source_text: Encapsulation of squaraines within macrocycles improves their quantum yields, increasing the quantum yield from 0.45 to 0.58 for 94.[102,107-109] Additionally, introduction of substituents that restrict torsional motions around the squaraine single bonds, as seen with 99, increases quantum yields. Phenols likely form hydrogen-bonds with the squaraine carbonyl groups, reducing the nonradiative decay, and increasing the quantum yield from 0.041 to 0.415.

page_payload.source_text: Squaraine-rotaxanes are increasingly being utilized in biological imaging applications and are now available for purchase from SETA. These fluorophores have demonstrated low quenching effects when conjugated to biomolecules, along with exceptional photo- and chemical stability, particularly in single molecule microscopy.[110] Despite the progress made, there is still significant potential for exploring and fine-tuning the photophysical properties of squaraine-rotaxanes. As a result, we anticipate a growing surge in their usage and further advancements in this field.

page_payload.source_text: Cycloparaphenylenes (CPPs) are cyclic arrangements of paralinked phenylenes, resembling a slice of a carbon nanotube (Figure 14). Their cyclic structure provides enhanced solubility and distinct optical properties, setting them apart from linear fluorophores. All sizes of CPPs share a common absorption, making them ideal candidates as multiplexing dyes. Unlike planar fluorophores, CPPs exhibit a red-shift in fluorescence by reducing the number of phenylenes in the hoop. For example, [12]CPP (103) emits at 450\mathrm{nm} , whereas [7]CPP (101) emits at 587\mathrm{nm} . For these strained aromatic macrocycles, the important factors controlling the HOMO and LUMO levels are torsion angles between neighboring phenylenes and phenylene bending.[111] The smaller the CPP, the more bent the phenylene rings become, resulting in destabilization of the HOMO and stabilization of the LUMO. As CPP size is reduced, the torsion angle between neighboring benzene rings decreases. This results in an increase in \pi -conjugation, therefore, stabilizing the LUMO and destabilizing the HOMO. Together, bending and torsion effects result in a red-shift in fluorescence as the CPP size decreases.

page_payload.translation_unit_protected_source_text: Cycloparaphenylenes (CPPs) are cyclic arrangements of paralinked phenylenes, resembling a slice of a carbon nanotube (Figure 14). Their cyclic structure provides enhanced solubility and distinct optical properties, setting them apart from linear fluorophores. All sizes of CPPs share a common absorption, making them ideal candidates as multiplexing dyes. Unlike planar fluorophores, CPPs exhibit a red-shift in fluorescence by reducing the number of phenylenes in the hoop. For example, [12]CPP (103) emits at 450\mathrm{nm} , whereas [7]CPP (101) emits at 587\mathrm{nm} . For these strained aromatic macrocycles, the important factors controlling the HOMO and LUMO levels are torsion angles between neighboring phenylenes and phenylene bending.[111] The smaller the CPP, the more bent the phenylene rings become, resulting in destabilization of the HOMO and stabilization of the LUMO. As CPP size is reduced, the torsion angle between neighboring benzene rings decreases. This results in an increase in \pi -conjugation, therefore, stabilizing the LUMO and destabilizing the HOMO. Together, bending and torsion effects result in a red-shift in fluorescence as the CPP size decreases.
page_payload.translation_unit_protected_translated_te: Cycloparaphenylene (CPPs) are ring structures composed of para-connected phenylene units, resembling a slice of a carbon nanotube (Figure 14). Their ring structure provides higher solubility and unique optical properties, distinguishing them from linear fluorophores. CPPs of all sizes share common absorption features, making them ideal candidates for multiplexed fluorescent dyes. Unlike planar fluorophores, CPPs exhibit fluorescence redshift by reducing the number of phenylene units in the ring. For example, [12]CPP(103) has an emission wavelength of 450 nm, while [7]CPP(101) has an emission wavelength of 587 nm. For these strained aromatic macrocycles, the key factors controlling HOMO and LUMO levels are the torsion angle between adjacent phenylene units and the degree of bending of the phenylene units.[111] The smaller the CPP size, the greater the bending of the phenylene rings, leading to a raised HOMO and lowered LUMO. As CPP size decreases, the torsion angle between adjacent phenyl rings decreases. This leads to enhanced $\pi$ conjugation, stabilizing the LUMO and raising the HOMO. The bending and torsion effects work together so that as CPP size decreases,, the fluorescence undergoes a redshift.

Unlike planar fluorophores, CPPs exhibit fluorescence redshift by reducing the number of phenylene units in the ring. For example, [12]CPP(103) has an emission wavelength of 450 nm, while [7]CPP(101) has an emission wavelength of 587 nm. For these strained aromatic macrocycles, the key factors controlling HOMO and LUMO levels are the torsion angle between adjacent phenylene units and the degree of bending of the phenylene units.[111] The smaller the CPP size, the greater the bending of the phenylene rings, leading to a raised HOMO and lowered LUMO. As CPP size decreases, the torsion angle between adjacent phenyl rings decreases. This leads to enhanced $\pi$ conjugation, stabilizing the LUMO and raising the HOMO. The bending and torsion effects work together so that as CPP size decreases,, the fluorescence undergoes a redshift.

page_payload.translated_text: Cycloparaphenylene (CPPs) are ring structures composed of para-connected phenylene units, resembling a slice of a carbon nanotube (Figure 14). Their ring structure provides higher solubility and unique optical properties, distinguishing them from linear fluorophores. CPPs of all sizes share common absorption features, making them ideal candidates for multiplexed fluorescent dyes. Unlike planar fluorophores, CPPs exhibit fluorescence redshift by reducing the number of phenylene units in the ring. For example, [12]CPP(103) has an emission wavelength of 450 nm, while [7]CPP(101) has an emission wavelength of 587 nm. For these strained aromatic macrocycles, the key factors controlling HOMO and LUMO levels are the torsion angle between adjacent phenylene units and the degree of bending of the phenylene units.[111] The smaller the CPP size, the greater the bending of the phenylene rings, leading to a raised HOMO and lowered LUMO. As CPP size decreases, the torsion angle between adjacent phenyl rings decreases. This leads to enhanced $\pi$ conjugation, stabilizing the LUMO and raising the HOMO. The bending and torsion effects work together so that as CPP size decreases,, the fluorescence undergoes a redshift.

page_payload.source_text: However, when CPPs become too small, they are no longer fluorescent due to symmetry rules.[112] Disrupting the centrosymmetry by introducing a kink into the structure, meta[n]CPPs, allows fluorescence in all sizes. [6]CPP is nonfluorescent, whereas m[6] CPP (102) fluoresces at 510 \mathrm{~nm} .

page_payload.translation_unit_protected_translated_te: However, when cycloparaphenylene (CPPs) are too small, they no longer fluoresce due to symmetry rules. By introducing kinks into the structure to break central symmetry, i.e., meta-substituted [n]CPPs, all sizes of CPPs can fluoresce. For example, [6]CPP does not emit light, while meta-[6]CPP(102) fluoresces at 510 nm.

By introducing kinks into the structure to break central symmetry, i.e., meta-substituted [n]CPPs, all sizes of CPPs can fluoresce. For example, [6]CPP does not emit light, while meta-[6]CPP(102) fluoresces at 510 nm.

By introducing kinks into the structure to break central symmetry, i.e., meta-substituted [n]CPPs, all sizes of CPPs can fluoresce. page_payload.translated_text: However, when cycloparaphenylene (CPPs) are too small, they no longer fluoresce due to symmetry rules. By introducing kinks into the structure to break central symmetry, i.e., meta-substituted [n]CPPs, all sizes of CPPs can fluoresce. For example, [6]CPP does not emit light, while meta-[6]CPP(102) fluoresces at 510 nm.

page_payload.source_text: CPPs are essentially unaffected by electron donating and withdrawing group incorporation on their scaffold, since their HOMO and LUMO orbitals are evenly distributed. For CPPs, electron accepting moieties must instead be incorporated into the backbone.[118] Adding a tetracyanoanthraquinone unit (105) to [10]CPP (103) shifts fluorescence dramatically to 642\mathrm{nm} , though with a decreased quantum yield.[117] However, if the electron acceptor is switched to a benzothiadiazole (106) the fluorescence is shifted to 571\mathrm{nm} and the quantum yield is retained.[115] Furthermore, multiple benzothiadiazole units can further shift the fluorescence.[119] Numerous alternative and promising acceptors, as well as donors, have the potential to shift fluorescence into the near-infrared range; nevertheless, further synthesis is required to substantiate these possibilities.[118]

page_payload.translation_unit_protected_source_text: CPPs are essentially unaffected by electron donating and withdrawing group incorporation on their scaffold, since their HOMO and LUMO orbitals are evenly distributed. For CPPs, electron accepting moieties must instead be incorporated into the backbone.[118] Adding a tetracyanoanthraquinone unit (105) to [10]CPP (103) shifts fluorescence dramatically to 642\mathrm{nm} , though with a decreased quantum yield.[117] However, if the electron acceptor is switched to a benzothiadiazole (106) the fluorescence is shifted to 571\mathrm{nm} and the quantum yield is retained.[115] Furthermore, multiple benzothiadiazole units can further shift the fluorescence.[119] Numerous alternative and promising acceptors, as well as donors, have the potential to shift fluorescence into the near-infrared range; nevertheless, further synthesis is required to substantiate these possibilities.[118]
page_payload.translation_unit_protected_translated_te: Since the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of CPPs are uniformly distributed, introducing electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups on the scaffold has minimal effect. For CPPs, electron-accepting units must be integrated into the backbone.[118] Adding a tetracyanoanthraquinone unit (105) to [10]CPP (103) can significantly red-shift fluorescence to 642 nm, but with reduced quantum yield.[117] However, replacing the electron acceptor with benzothiadiazole (106) shifts fluorescence to 571 nm while maintaining quantum yield.[115] Additionally, introducing multiple benzothiadiazole units can further shift fluorescence emission.[119] Many other promising acceptor and donor units are expected to shift fluorescence into the near-infrared region; further synthetic work is needed to verify these possibilities.[118]
page_payload.translation_unit_translated_text: Since the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of CPPs are uniformly distributed, introducing electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups on the scaffold has minimal effect. For CPPs, electron-accepting units must be integrated into the backbone.[118] Adding a tetracyanoanthraquinone unit (105) to [10]CPP (103) can significantly red-shift fluorescence to 642 nm, but with reduced quantum yield.[117] However, replacing the electron acceptor with benzothiadiazole (106) shifts fluorescence to 571 nm while maintaining quantum yield.[115] Additionally, introducing multiple benzothiadiazole units can further shift fluorescence emission.[119] Many other promising acceptor and donor units are expected to shift fluorescence into the near-infrared region; further synthetic work is needed to verify these possibilities.[118]

For CPPs, electron-accepting units must be integrated into the backbone.[118] Adding a tetracyanoanthraquinone unit (105) to [10]CPP (103) can significantly red-shift fluorescence to 642 nm, but with reduced quantum yield.[117] However, replacing the electron acceptor with benzothiadiazole (106) shifts fluorescence to 571 nm while maintaining quantum yield.[115] Additionally, introducing multiple benzothiadiazole units can further shift fluorescence emission.[119] Many other promising acceptor and donor units are expected to shift fluorescence into the near-infrared region; further synthetic work is needed to verify these possibilities.[118]

For CPPs, electron-accepting units must be integrated into the backbone.[118] Adding a tetracyanoanthraquinone unit (105) to [10]CPP (103) can significantly red-shift fluorescence to 642 nm, but with reduced quantum yield.[117] However, replacing the electron acceptor with benzothiadiazole (106) shifts fluorescence to 571 nm while maintaining quantum yield.[115] Additionally, introducing multiple benzothiadiazole units can further shift fluorescence emission.[119] Many other promising acceptor and donor units are expected to shift fluorescence into the near-infrared region; further synthetic work is needed to verify these possibilities.[118]

page_payload.source_text: To enhance quantum yields in CPPs, different methods than those above are required. Deviating CPPs from their centrosymmetric nature allows LUMO to HOMO fluorescence transitions, resulting in higher quantum yields. Therefore, the larger and less strained the [n] CPP, the higher the quantum yield (100 vs 101). Additionally, disrupting the centrosymmetry, as in meta[n]CPPs, increases the fluorescence by 5—65 fold.[116] [7]CPP (101) is minimally fluorescent, with a quantum yield of 0.007. Changing one phenylene connection from para- to metaconnectivity yields m[7] CPP (104) with a quantum yield of 0.45.[116] Lastly, it is important to avoid ICT as it dramatically decreases nanohoop quantum yield.[115] While the quantum yield of 106 remains the same as the parent compound (103) because they fluoresce through the same mechanism, 105, on the other hand, experiences ICT, resulting in a decrease in the quantum yield.

page_payload.translation_unit_protected_source_text: To enhance quantum yields in CPPs, different methods than those above are required. Deviating CPPs from their centrosymmetric nature allows LUMO to HOMO fluorescence transitions, resulting in higher quantum yields. Therefore, the larger and less strained the [n] CPP, the higher the quantum yield (100 vs 101). Additionally, disrupting the centrosymmetry, as in meta[n]CPPs, increases the fluorescence by 5—65 fold.[116] [7]CPP (101) is minimally fluorescent, with a quantum yield of 0.007. Changing one phenylene connection from para- to metaconnectivity yields m[7] CPP (104) with a quantum yield of 0.45.[116] Lastly, it is important to avoid ICT as it dramatically decreases nanohoop quantum yield.[115] While the quantum yield of 106 remains the same as the parent compound (103) because they fluoresce through the same mechanism, 105, on the other hand, experiences ICT, resulting in a decrease in the quantum yield.
page_payload.translation_unit_protected_translated_te: To improve the quantum yield of cycloparaphenylene (CPPs), new strategies different from the above methods are needed. By deviating CPPs from their central symmetry, fluorescence transitions from LUMO to HOMO can be achieved, resulting in higher quantum yields. Therefore, the larger the [n]CPP size, the lower the ring strain, and the higher the quantum yield (e.g., 100 and 101 vs). Additionally, breaking central symmetry (as in meta-[n]CPPs) can enhance fluorescence by 5 to 65 times.[116] [7]CPP(101) has extremely weak fluorescence with a quantum yield of only 0.007. Changing one phenyl ring connection from para to meta yields m[7]CPP(104), with quantum yield increased to 0.45.[116] Finally, intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) should be avoided, as it significantly reduces the quantum yield of nanorings.[115] Although 106 has the same quantum yield as its parent compound 103 (due to the same fluorescence mechanism), 105 undergoes ICT, leading to reduced quantum yield.

Therefore, the larger the [n]CPP size, the lower the ring strain, and the higher the quantum yield (e.g., 100 and 101 vs). Additionally, breaking central symmetry (as in meta-[n]CPPs) can enhance fluorescence by 5 to 65 times.[116] [7]CPP(101) has extremely weak fluorescence with a quantum yield of only 0.007. Changing one phenyl ring connection from para to meta yields m[7]CPP(104), with quantum yield increased to 0.45.[116] Finally, intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) should be avoided, as it significantly reduces the quantum yield of nanorings.[115] Although 106 has the same quantum yield as its parent compound 103 (due to the same fluorescence mechanism), 105 undergoes ICT, leading to reduced quantum yield.

page_payload.translated_text: To improve the quantum yield of cycloparaphenylene (CPPs), new strategies different from the above methods are needed. By deviating CPPs from their central symmetry, fluorescence transitions from LUMO to HOMO can be achieved, resulting in higher quantum yields. Therefore, the larger the [n]CPP size, the lower the ring strain, and the higher the quantum yield (e.g., 100 and 101 vs). Additionally, breaking central symmetry (as in meta-[n]CPPs) can enhance fluorescence by 5 to 65 times.[116] [7]CPP(101) has extremely weak fluorescence with a quantum yield of only 0.007. Changing one phenyl ring connection from para to meta yields m[7]CPP(104), with quantum yield increased to 0.45.[116] Finally, intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) should be avoided, as it significantly reduces the quantum yield of nanorings.[115] Although 106 has the same quantum yield as its parent compound 103 (due to the same fluorescence mechanism), 105 undergoes ICT, leading to reduced quantum yield.

page_payload.source_text: CPPs have shown potential as fluorophores for biological applications.[120-122] With recent advancements in functionalization, synthesis, and understanding of their photophysical properties, we expect that these distinctive structures to be further explored and adopted as fluorophores for fluorescence imaging. Their unique structure opens exciting opportunities, such as utilizing their cavities for supramolecular recognition and creating rotaxane dyes. CPPs hold promise as candidates for various imaging techniques in biological contexts.

page_payload.source_text: Fluorophores play a critical role in biology and biochemistry. Small molecule fluorophores are advantageous because precise manipulation of their structure alters their photophysical properties. We explored various methods for tuning fluorescence and examined fundamental physical organic chemistry concepts that govern these properties.

replay.source_preview: Encapsulation of squaraines within macrocycles improves their quantum yields, increasing the quantum yield from 0.45 to 0.58 for 94.[102,107-109] Additionally, introduction of substituents that restrict torsional motion…

saved_item.source_text: Encapsulation of squaraines within macrocycles improves their quantum yields, increasing the quantum yield from 0.45 to 0.58 for 94.[102,107-109] Additionally, introduction of substituents that restrict torsional motions around the squaraine single bonds, as seen with 99, increases quantum yields. Phenols likely form hydrogen-bonds with the squaraine carbonyl groups, reducing the nonradiative decay, and increasing the quantum yield from 0.041 to 0.415.

replay_result.translated_text: Encapsulating squaraine dyes within macrocycles improves their quantum yield, e.g., compound 94's quantum yield increased from 0.45 to 0.58.[102,107-109] Additionally, introducing substituents that restrict torsional motion around squaraine single bonds (as seen with compound 99) also improves quantum yield. Phenols likely form hydrogen bonds with squaraine carbonyl groups, reducing nonradiative decay and increasing quantum yield from 0.041 to 0.415.
